title: Multi-flux Aharonov-Bohm caging with tunable couplings
authors: Le-Chuan Wang, Sai Li, Jia Liu, Zheng-Yuan Xue
abstract: Aharonov-Bohm (AB) caging is the complete wavefunction localization effect in translational-invariant lattices induced by destructive phase interference. These phases originate from the gauge fields such as the penetrated magnetic fields, which are directly related to several novel topological quantum states of matter. Recently, this effect has demonstrated significant potential for applications in quantum simulation and topological quantum computation. Here, we propose a scalable protocol to derive universal conditions for AB caging with multi-flux. The numerical simulations validate the theoretical predictions by directly observing AB caging phenomena. We also investigate the breakage of the caging effect with onsite detuning. Our protocol can be directly tested in several quantum many-body platforms and provides an alternative approach for advancing quantum simulation of exotic state matter.
summary: This paper proposes a method to achieve Aharonov-Bohm caging in lattices with multiple flux parameters, allowing for highly tunable wavefunction localization. It demonstrates that while this caging is a powerful tool for quantum simulation, it is sensitive to environmental perturbations like noise and dissipation. The findings are applicable to various platforms including photonics, superconducting qubits, and trapped ions.
main_problem: Investigating the universal conditions for Aharonov-Bohm (AB) caging in multi-flux lattices and exploring how this localization effect is destroyed by disorder, dissipation, and non-Hermitian effects.
main_result: The authors derive universal conditions for AB caging in multi-flux lattices and demonstrate that while the effect is robust to certain configurations, it is cumulatively destroyed by increasing on-site detuning, dissipation, or non-Hermitian hopping.
method: The study uses theoretical derivation of eigenvalue equations in k-space combined with numerical simulations of photon dynamics and Lindblad master equations to analyze localization and transport.
model_or_system: A 1D multi-flux lattice with tunable couplings and gauge fields, applicable to photonic lattices, superconducting circuits (circuit QED), and trapped-ion systems.
key_observables: Inverse Participation Number (IPN), IPN fluctuation (sigma), and group velocity (vg).
important_parameters: Transition phases (phi), coupling strength (J), disorder strength (Delta), dissipation rate (gamma), and non-Hermitian hopping strength (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The physical realizations assume the validity of the rotating-wave approximation (RWA).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice structure; Fig 2 displays numerical simulations of photon localization and transport; Fig 5 shows IPN time evolution under disorder; Fig 6 and 7 present phase diagrams and statistical localization criteria.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the AB caging concept, proposes a scalable protocol for multi-flux caging, validates it via numerical simulations, investigates the impact of disorder and dissipation, and discusses experimental implementations in quantum platforms.
why_interesting: It provides a scalable framework for engineering flat-band localization and explores the 'inverse Anderson transition' where disorder enables transport, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The interaction between the lattice sites An, Cn,i and An+1 is shown in the figure, where the solid line represents the strength to be a constant J. The dashed line indicates that there is a transition phase e−iϕn,i in the coupling between the lattice, and there are N paths(Cn,i ) between the adjacent sites An and An+1. The transmis- sion process of photons in the lattice is along the z direction.

FIG. 2. The lattice system we studied consists of 9 unit cells, 49 sites in total, and the initial state of |Ai⟩= |0, 0...1i...0⟩excited in the lattice. (a) The dynamics process of photon in the lattice reveals the perfect localization phenomenon; (b) The AB caging effect is absent, and the photon presents a transport process in the lattice; (c) The evolution law of IPN with the passage of time; (d) The IPN changes according to the time when the AB caging occurs.

FIG. 4. Time evolution of IPN for the different disoder strength. The time evolution of IPN for the case: (a) ∆= J and (b) ∆= 2J. The dynamics of photons in the lattice for the case: (c) ∆= J and (d) ∆= 2J.

FIG. 3. (a) By regulating the strength of disorder, the evolution pro- cess of the IPN according to time is observed. (b) The stereoscopic picture in (a) is transformed into a plane picture for observation, and the cage effect will be destroyed quickly when the strength of the disorder increases, at last the AB caging effect will disappaer.

FIG. 5. Time evolution of IPN for the different disoder strength and each case is calculated with 500 disorder realiztions. The time evolu- tion of IPN for the case: (a) ∆is randomly distributed between 0 and 2J. (b) ∆is randomly distributed between 0 and 4J. The dynamics of photons in the lattice for the case: (c) ∆is randomly distributed between 0 and 2J. (d) ∆is randomly distributed between 0 and 4J.
